title: Defending Against a Phishing Attack
description: You are a Blue Team SOC analyst. Suspicious activity has been flagged. Investigate and respond to a phishing campaign targeting your organization.

phases.name: Phase 1: Initial Detection
phases.narrative: Your SIEM has triggered an alert: 'Multiple failed O365 login attempts detected for CFO account from unusual geographic location.' The timestamp shows this began 10 minutes ago. What do you do first?

logs.event: Failed login attempt - user: jsmith@company.com - IP: 185.220.101.45 (TOR exit node) - Location: Romania
Failed login attempt - user: jsmith@company.com - IP: 185.220.101.45 - Attempt 2/5
Inbound email flagged: Subject='Invoice #4521 - Urgent' Attachment: Invoice_4521.xlsm - Sender: vendor@acme-supplies.net (DMARC FAIL)
Suspicious macro execution detected in Excel process on DESKTOP-CFO01

choices.text: Immediately block the suspicious IP in the firewall and investigate the logs
choices.outcome: Good immediate response! The TOR exit node IP is blocked. You review logs and discover the DMARC-failing email with a macro-enabled attachment. The attack vector becomes clear.

choices.text: Continue monitoring without blocking - gather more intelligence
choices.outcome: Passive approach costs time. While you monitor, the CFO enables the macro. The attacker achieves persistence. Incident escalates. Early blocking would have prevented this.

choices.text: Immediately contact the CFO to verify the email and halt any action
choices.outcome: Excellent user communication! You call the CFO who confirms they received a suspicious invoice email but hasn't opened it yet. You've prevented execution at the last moment.

phases.name: Phase 2: Containment
phases.narrative: Investigation confirms a phishing email with a macro-enabled attachment was delivered to the CFO. The email bypassed DMARC. SIEM shows the CFO's machine (DESKTOP-CFO01) made an outbound connection to 185.220.101.45:4444. How do you contain this?

logs.event: OUTBOUND ALLOWED - src: 10.0.1.50 (DESKTOP-CFO01) - dst: 185.220.101.45:4444 - Proto: TCP - Bytes: 2048
Process tree: EXCEL.EXE -> cmd.exe -> powershell.exe -enc [base64 payload]

choices.text: Immediately isolate DESKTOP-CFO01 from the network and disable the account
choices.outcome: Correct! Network isolation cuts the C2 channel. Account disable prevents credential misuse. You've contained the threat and preserved evidence for forensics.

choices.text: Block outbound port 4444 at the firewall only
choices.outcome: Partial containment. The C2 channel on 4444 is blocked, but the attacker pivots to port 443 (HTTPS). The endpoint remains compromised. Insufficient response.

choices.text: Immediately reimage the workstation without forensic capture
choices.outcome: Too hasty! While this removes the malware, you destroyed forensic evidence needed to understand the full scope of compromise. No artifacts remain for analysis.

phases.name: Phase 3: Eradication & Investigation
phases.narrative: The CFO's machine is isolated. Forensic analysis reveals the attacker established persistence via a registry run key. O365 audit logs show the attacker accessed the CFO's emails for 8 minutes before containment. What's your eradication strategy?

logs.event: Mailbox access: jsmith@company.com accessed by suspicious session - 47 emails read including subject 'Q4 Financial Report'
File download: SharePoint - 'Q4_Budget_2024.xlsx' downloaded via suspicious session
Scheduled task created: 'MicrosoftEdgeUpdate' running hourly - secondary persistence mechanism

choices.text: Reset CFO O365 password, revoke all sessions, remove registry keys, delete scheduled tasks, reimage workstation
choices.outcome: Comprehensive eradication! All persistence mechanisms removed. O365 access revoked. The attacker has been fully evicted. Excellent systematic approach.

choices.text: Reset the password and remove the registry key only
choices.outcome: Incomplete! The scheduled task secondary persistence remains active. The attacker regains access 1 hour later via the 'MicrosoftEdgeUpdate' task. Re-compromise occurred.

choices.text: Run AV scan and clean detected malware, keep the machine online
choices.outcome: AV removes the known malware but misses the scheduled task (fileless component). Rootkit persistence remains. The system appears clean but is still compromised.

phases.name: Phase 4: Recovery & Hardening
phases.narrative: The immediate threat is contained and eradicated. Now you need to prevent recurrence and restore normal operations. What hardening measures do you implement?

logs.event: Incident escalated to CISO - data exfiltration confirmed (financial documents)
Similar phishing emails found in 3 other employee inboxes - campaign confirmed
Threat intel feed updated - 185.220.101.45 added to blocklist

choices.text: Enforce MFA for all O365 accounts, implement DMARC enforcement, and conduct phishing awareness training
choices.outcome: Excellent preventive measures! MFA prevents credential compromise even if passwords are stolen. DMARC enforcement blocks spoofed emails. Training reduces human risk. Comprehensive hardening.

choices.text: Send a company-wide email warning about phishing
choices.outcome: Awareness helps but is insufficient alone. No technical controls implemented. The next phishing campaign could succeed just as easily. Minimal impact on security posture.

choices.text: Disable Office macros via Group Policy, implement attack surface reduction rules, enforce MFA
choices.outcome: Strong technical hardening! Disabling macros eliminates this attack vector entirely. ASR rules provide defense in depth. MFA protects credentials. Well-rounded response.

phases.name: Incident Resolved
phases.narrative: You have successfully investigated and responded to the phishing incident. A full incident report has been prepared for the CISO and affected parties have been notified.
